title: RAG
description: Build retrieval-augmented systems that ground model outputs with evidence.
outcomes: Compare vector indexes and retrieval topologies.
Evaluate grounding quality and hallucination risk.

lessons.title: What is RAG?
lessons.description: Understand how retrieval grounds model responses in data.
checklist: I can explain RAG in plain language.
I can identify when to use RAG.
I can list one common pitfall and fix.
questions: Why can retrieval reduce hallucinations?
How does RAG change model trust?

lessons.title: Chunking
lessons.description: Split long documents into useful retrieval units.
checklist: I can explain chunk size and overlap.
I can tune chunking for coherence and recall.
I can justify one chunking choice.
questions: What happens if chunks are too large?
When should chunk overlap increase?

lessons.title: Embeddings
lessons.description: Convert semantic meaning into vector search signals.
checklist: I can define what an embedding captures.
I can compare cosine and dot-product behavior.
I can choose embedding updates when data changes.
questions: Why does embedding drift matter over time?
How do embeddings impact recall?

lessons.title: Vector Databases
lessons.description: Store and query vectors for fast semantic retrieval.
checklist: I can compare indexing strategies.
I can measure query quality quickly.
I can plan refresh and delete operations.
questions: When is a vector database useful versus a cache?
What operations need stronger monitoring in production?

lessons.title: Retrieval
lessons.description: Select and rank relevant passages for prompt injection.
checklist: I can explain recall versus precision tradeoff.
I can inspect top-k quality.
I can avoid returning irrelevant chunks.
questions: How do you choose top-k for different applications?
What signals show poor retrieval quality?

lessons.title: Re-ranking
lessons.description: Improve final context quality before generation.
checklist: I can explain why first-pass retrieval is not always enough.
I can evaluate rerankers by quality signal.
I can reduce duplicate context.
questions: Why does reranking often improve answer quality?
What is over-retrieval and when is it useful?

lessons.title: Citations
lessons.description: Attach evidence links so outputs become auditable.
checklist: I can map claims to source snippets.
I can format stable citation ids.
I can handle missing evidence gracefully.
questions: Why do citations improve product trust?
What should a citation include at minimum?

lessons.title: Evaluation
lessons.description: Create RAG-specific correctness and grounding checks.
checklist: I can define retrieval-aware eval sets.
I can score grounding and citation accuracy.
I can track latency with quality tradeoffs.
questions: How do you evaluate grounding quality?
How do you measure retrieval-induced errors?

lessons.title: Mini Project: RAG Evidence Router
lessons.description: Build a mini retrieval pipeline with explicit fallback behavior.
checklist: I can demonstrate retrieve-and-rerank flow.
I can attach minimum evidence to answers.
I can define a no-evidence fallback.
questions: What failure happens when retrieval confidence drops?
How do you protect users from unsupported statements?

lessons.title: Portfolio Project: RAG Q&A
lessons.description: Build a production-style Q&A flow with citations and metrics.
checklist: I can build full ingest and query flow.
I can enforce citation requirements.
I can publish an eval dashboard summary.
questions: How will you monitor retrieval quality monthly?
What are your most important failure alerts?

lessons.title: RAG Quiz
checklist: I can explain RAG architecture end-to-end.
I can evaluate evidence quality.
I can design a safe fallback policy.
questions: When is retrieval quality the bottleneck?
How do citations affect user trust?

quizQuestions.prompt: Explain RAG in your own words and why it improves trust.
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: RAG adds external retrieval before generation so answers are grounded in sources, making behavior more auditable and reducing hallucinated facts.

options.label: Retrieval before generation
options.explanation: RAG adds external retrieval before generation so answers are grounded in sources, making behavior more auditable and reducing hallucinated facts.

options.label: Fine-tune first
options.explanation: RAG is often better when answers must use current, private, or changing source material.

options.label: Maximum chunk size
options.explanation: Huge chunks can dilute relevance, increase cost, and reduce answer quality.

options.label: Keyword search only
options.explanation: Keyword search can miss semantically similar content that uses different wording.

quizQuestions.prompt: How do chunk size and overlap affect retrieval quality?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Chunk size controls context granularity: too large can dilute signals, while too small fragments meaning and coherence; overlap helps preserve continuity.

options.label: Maximum chunk size
options.explanation: Huge chunks can dilute relevance, increase cost, and reduce answer quality.

options.label: Keyword search only
options.explanation: Keyword search can miss semantically similar content that uses different wording.

options.label: Confidence without citations
options.explanation: Citations help users verify claims and reduce unsupported generation.

options.label: Balanced context chunks
options.explanation: Chunk size controls context granularity: too large can dilute signals, while too small fragments meaning and coherence; overlap helps preserve continuity.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why do embeddings enable semantic matching over keyword matching?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Embeddings represent meaning in vector space, allowing similar concepts to be matched even when wording differs.

options.label: Keyword search only
options.explanation: Keyword search can miss semantically similar content that uses different wording.

options.label: Confidence without citations
options.explanation: Citations help users verify claims and reduce unsupported generation.

options.label: Vector similarity matching
options.explanation: Embeddings represent meaning in vector space, allowing similar concepts to be matched even when wording differs.

options.label: Retrieval equals reranking
options.explanation: Retrieval finds candidates; reranking reorders them for relevance before generation.

quizQuestions.prompt: What operational concerns come with vector database scale?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: At scale, indexing strategy, update pipelines, and monitoring index drift become critical to keep retrieval correct and fast.

options.label: Confidence without citations
options.explanation: Citations help users verify claims and reduce unsupported generation.

options.label: Retrieval equals reranking
options.explanation: Retrieval finds candidates; reranking reorders them for relevance before generation.

options.label: Indexing, updates, drift
options.explanation: At scale, indexing strategy, update pipelines, and monitoring index drift become critical to keep retrieval correct and fast.

options.label: Answer length metric
options.explanation: RAG quality depends on retrieval relevance, groundedness, citation quality, and user success.

quizQuestions.prompt: Why is reranking a separate step from retrieval?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Reranking refines initial candidates with stronger relevance signals before prompt injection, improving final context quality.

options.label: Retrieval equals reranking
options.explanation: Retrieval finds candidates; reranking reorders them for relevance before generation.

options.label: Answer length metric
options.explanation: RAG quality depends on retrieval relevance, groundedness, citation quality, and user success.

options.label: Fine-tune first
options.explanation: RAG is often better when answers must use current, private, or changing source material.

options.label: Relevance refinement step
options.explanation: Reranking refines initial candidates with stronger relevance signals before prompt injection, improving final context quality.

quizQuestions.prompt: How do citations reduce hallucination risk?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Citations force outputs to be tied to concrete evidence and make unsupported claims visible and auditable.

options.label: Answer length metric
options.explanation: RAG quality depends on retrieval relevance, groundedness, citation quality, and user success.

options.label: Fine-tune first
options.explanation: RAG is often better when answers must use current, private, or changing source material.

options.label: Evidence-backed answers
options.explanation: Citations force outputs to be tied to concrete evidence and make unsupported claims visible and auditable.

options.label: Maximum chunk size
options.explanation: Huge chunks can dilute relevance, increase cost, and reduce answer quality.

quizQuestions.prompt: What metrics should you track for RAG quality?
quizQuestions.correctAnswer: Track grounding accuracy, retrieval precision/recall, citation validity, and latency because all affect trust and usability.

options.label: Grounding and retrieval metrics
options.explanation: Track grounding accuracy, retrieval precision/recall, citation validity, and latency because all affect trust and usability.

options.label: Fine-tune first
options.explanation: RAG is often better when answers must use current, private, or changing source material.

options.label: Maximum chunk size
options.explanation: Huge chunks can dilute relevance, increase cost, and reduce answer quality.

options.label: Keyword search only
options.explanation: Keyword search can miss semantically similar content that uses different wording.
